techniques.evidence_quote: we observed an attacker targeting an AWS environment with a long-term access key (AKIA)
credentials generated are separate from the original compromised IAM identity, making it more difficult to track the attacker

techniques.technique_name: Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location
techniques.evidence_quote: create a new role named `SupportAWS`.This shows the attacker using names that look legitimate to avoid detections
targeting an AWS environment with a long-term access key (AKIA)
creating an IAMuser, `supdev`, with a login profile that had the AdministratorAccess policy attached
attached the policy `AdministratorAccess` to the `AWSSupport` role
attached the policy `AdministratorAccess` to the `AWSSupport` role. This ensures the role will have the requisite privileges
the attacker navigated to the AWS SES service, potentially checking the capabilities of the SES service

attack_chain.description: Attacker gained access to the victim's AWS environment using a compromised long-term IAM access key (AKIA).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: we observed an attacker targeting an AWS environment with a long-term access key (AKIA)

evidence_chunks.quote: we observed an attacker targeting an AWS environment with a long-term access key (AKIA)
we observed an attacker targeting an AWS environment with a long-term access key (AKIA)

attack_chain.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
attack_chain.description: Called sts:GetFederationToken to generate temporary credentials with a wildcard allow-all policy, converting CLI access into separately-scoped federated credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: One of the first actions was to call `GetFederationToken`

evidence_chunks.quote: One of the first actions was to call `GetFederationToken`
One of the first actions was to call `GetFederationToken`

attack_chain.description: Used GetSigninToken and ConsoleLogin to pivot from CLI to AWS Console access as federated-user/admin.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: generated an AWS Console sign-in link using GetSigninToken

evidence_chunks.quote: generated an AWS Console sign-in link using GetSigninToken
generated an AWS Console sign-in link using GetSigninToken

attack_chain.description: Created a new IAM role named `SupportAWS` with an assume-role policy trusting attacker-controlled AWS account 713521355166.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: create a new role named `SupportAWS` ... attached a policy to that role that allows them to assume it from an attacker-controlled account `713521355166`

attack_chain.description: Attached AWS managed AdministratorAccess policy to the SupportAWS role to grant full privileges.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: attached the policy `AdministratorAccess` to the `AWSSupport` role

evidence_chunks.quote: attached the policy `AdministratorAccess` to the `AWSSupport` role
attached the policy `AdministratorAccess` to the `AWSSupport` role

attack_chain.description: From attacker-controlled account 713521355166 (user adminprod), assumed the SupportAWS role via SwitchRole/AssumeRole to access the victim environment from outside.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attacker then assumed the role via the `713521355166` account, authenticated as an IAM user named `adminprod`

attack_chain.description: Using the SupportAWS role, created a new IAM user `supdev` as an additional persistence layer.
Created a login profile (console password) for the supdev user via CreateLoginProfile.
Attached AdministratorAccess managed policy to the supdev user via AttachUserPolicy.
Logged in to the AWS console as supdev twice from two different regions within a short time period.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: pivoted to the user `supdev`, accessing the console twice from two different regions in a short time period

evidence_chunks.quote: pivoted to the user `supdev`, accessing the console twice from two different regions in a short time period
pivoted to the user `supdev`, accessing the console twice from two different regions in a short time period

attack_chain.description: Enumerated AWS SES configuration (GetAccount, ListEmailIdentities, ListIdentities, GetSendQuota) to determine sandbox status and sending limits.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: navigated to the AWS SES service, potentially checking the capabilities of the SES service

evidence_chunks.quote: navigated to the AWS SES service, potentially checking the capabilities of the SES service
navigated to the AWS SES service, potentially checking the capabilities of the SES service

attack_chain.description: The following day, from the original long-term access key, the same IP called GetAccount across multiple regions, indicating automated tooling.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The following day we observed the same IP attempting the `GetAccount` API call across multiple regions from the original long-term access key

evidence_chunks.quote: The following day we observed the same IP attempting the `GetAccount` API call across multiple regions from the original long-term access key
The following day we observed the same IP attempting the `GetAccount` API call across multiple regions from the original long-term access key

initial_access_vector: Compromised long-term IAM access key (AKIA) used to authenticate to the victim AWS account.
persistence_mechanism: Created a cross-account-assumable IAM role `SupportAWS` trusting attacker-owned AWS account 713521355166 with AdministratorAccess; additionally created IAM user `supdev` with a login profile and AdministratorAccess policy.
privilege_escalation: Attached AWS managed AdministratorAccess policy to the attacker-created role and to the new IAM user supdev, granting full administrative privileges.
